evidence_quotes.quote: What we see now is that especially with games, some of them are being killed off.

paragraphs.text: First of all, thank you for putting in the effort in making this digital fairness act, I greatly appreciate the work that you do. I would like to give some feedback on this act. I believe one important pillar of digital fairness is that when we purchase software or games, we should own it. Of course, it isnt always possible when part of the software is a service. However, if you pay money for some software or a game, the main functionality that you use it for should be yours. What we see now is that especially with games, some of them are being killed off. This is to say that the live service of the game is being ceased, and sometimes even functionality that could have continued working without any problem. What this means is that the game will be virtually unplayable, despite sometimes having completely functional single player functionality. When you buy a game, you should own that game and be able to play anything that does not need to rely on live service to function, even after this live service ceases to exist. Next to that, it is completely feasible in my vision (as a software developer), that developers of games can define ways in which the functionality of their games may be able to be continued, without requiring the life service of that company specifically. This can be done in many ways and will depend on the game and the systems in place. However, companies currently do not need to do anything and are capable of killing games without providing any such means. I believe this unilateral decision on the side of the company to kill off games and to remove functionality that would otherwise be completely playable should be in violation of digital fairness. How exactly this would be put into a law is something that is outside the expertise that I have, but I hope that by providing this feedback, you are able to hopefully consider what I am saying and see whether it is feasible to include something about it in this law. In any case, I thank you for your time and effort, both reading this feedback and working on these new legislations.
